quiz_questions.question_text: You receive a 10% raise on a $50,000 salary, giving you $5,000 more per year. Which allocation strategy builds the most long-term wealth?

options.text: Use it all for lifestyle upgrades since you earned it
Split it: increase your savings rate with about half and allow modest lifestyle improvements with the rest
Put 100% into savings and make no lifestyle changes at all
Add it all to your checking account and decide later

quiz_questions.explanation: A balanced approach avoids 'lifestyle creep' (spending every raise) while staying sustainable. Saving 100% sounds ideal but often isn't maintained long-term. Having no plan (leaving it in checking) typically results in unintentional spending. The 50/50 split builds wealth while rewarding your progress.

quiz_questions.question_text: After an unexpected job loss, which set of actions should be taken first?

options.text: Immediately take on credit card debt to maintain your current lifestyle
Cut discretionary spending, file for unemployment benefits, assess your emergency fund runway, and begin seeking new income
Wait several months before making any changes to see if the situation resolves
Withdraw all retirement savings to cover expenses right away

quiz_questions.explanation: The priority during job loss is to extend your financial runway: reduce non-essential spending, claim available benefits, calculate how long your emergency fund lasts, and actively seek new income. Borrowing on credit cards creates high-interest debt. Withdrawing retirement funds incurs penalties and taxes. Waiting too long depletes savings unnecessarily.

quiz_questions.question_text: A baby adds approximately _____ per year in expenses.
How does marriage most significantly affect a household budget?

options.text: Only the tax filing status changes
Combined income, shared expenses (potential savings on housing/insurance), and the need to align financial goals all change the entire financial picture
Only housing costs are affected since you share a home
Budgets remain separate and independent after marriage

quiz_questions.explanation: Marriage creates a financial partnership that affects nearly every budget category: combined incomes change cash flow, shared housing/insurance can reduce costs, but spending habits may conflict. Tax status changes (married filing jointly can lower rates), and goals must be aligned (savings targets, retirement plans, major purchases).

quiz_questions.question_text: Buying a home changes the budget by adding:

options.text: Mortgage + property taxes + insurance + maintenance (1-3% of home value/year) + utilities increase

quiz_questions.question_text: You receive a $3,000 tax refund. Which approach maximizes your financial position?

options.text: Spend it on a vacation since it feels like 'bonus' money
Apply it strategically: fill emergency fund first, then pay high-interest debt, then invest the remainder
Put it all in a low-interest savings account for safekeeping
Split it equally among all financial goals regardless of priority

quiz_questions.explanation: A windfall is most powerful when applied to your highest-priority financial need: an incomplete emergency fund (prevents future debt), then high-interest debt (saves interest), then investments (compound growth). Treating it as 'bonus money' for spending wastes its wealth-building potential. A strategic priority order outperforms equal splitting.

quiz_questions.question_text: Retirement budget adjustments include:

options.text: Potentially lower income, no commute costs, higher healthcare costs, and shifting from saving to spending mode

quiz_questions.question_text: Health crisis financial impact can include:

options.text: Medical bills + reduced income + increased insurance + potential disability — emergency fund and insurance are critical

quiz_questions.question_text: When transitioning from two incomes to one (e.g., parental leave):

options.text: Budget BEFORE the change: test living on one income for 3-6 months while saving the second income

quiz_questions.question_text: Divorce typically reduces household wealth by _____ due to splitting assets and maintaining two households.
Moving to a higher-cost city requires:

options.text: Recalculating ALL budget categories: housing (+30-100%), transportation, food, and adjusting savings goals

quiz_questions.question_text: Annual inflation of 3% means budgets must increase by _____ per year just to maintain the same lifestyle.

options.text: 3% (expenses naturally rise — budget must account for this)

quiz_questions.question_text: A car accident requiring $5,000 in repairs (with $1,000 deductible) affects your budget for _____ months if absorbing from monthly budget.

options.text: 2-4 months (depending on how much 'flex' money exists monthly — this is why emergency funds matter)

quiz_questions.question_text: After paying off a $300/month student loan, the freed-up money should:

options.text: Be redirected to the next financial priority (invest, next debt, or increase savings — don't let it leak into spending)

quiz_questions.question_text: Periodic budget reviews should check:

options.text: Income, expenses, savings rate, debt progress, goal alignment, and any upcoming life changes

quiz_questions.question_text: A promotion bringing $10,000 more annually creates the most wealth if _____ is saved/invested.

options.text: At least 50% — ideally even more, since you were already living on less before the raise

quiz_questions.question_text: Having a child ages 0-17 costs approximately _____ total (2024, middle-income family).
A financial buffer in the budget (5-10% unassigned) helps because:

options.text: It absorbs small unexpected expenses without derailing the entire budget — reducing stress and maintaining consistency

quiz_questions.question_text: The most successful long-term budgeters:

options.text: Regularly review and adjust their budget as life changes — flexibility keeps the plan relevant and motivating

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, budget adjustments demonstrate that personal finance is:

options.text: A dynamic, ongoing process requiring regular application of financial math concepts to changing real-world circumstances
